Q1: I tried to keep things consistent, usually studying in the evenings after my part-time job. I'm a hands-on learner, so I'd spread out my notes and physically work through problems rather than just reading. With 20 online courses to juggle, I had to be somewhat organized, but honestly it felt overwhelming most of the time. I'd block out maybe two hours a day on weekdays, but it never felt like enough given everything I was taking on.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was due. I tried to have a regular schedule, but with limited resources and working to support myself, deadlines often dictated when I could actually sit down and focus. Some weeks I'd be more consistent, other weeks everything would pile up and I'd have to prioritize whatever was most urgent. It wasn't ideal, but it was survival mode a lot of the time.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I knew an assignment was coming up or when I needed to check what videos I still had to watch. Sometimes I'd get an email reminder about something due and that would prompt me to go check. I wasn't someone who logged in just to browse around—it was always purpose-driven because I had so little free time. Fear of missing something important was honestly a big motivator.
Q4: There was a brief period mid-semester where logging in after dinner became almost automatic. I'd finish eating, wash up, and then open the LMS before doing anything else. But that routine fell apart when things got really stressful and I started feeling scared about failing. Once the anxiety crept in, the routine felt more like a burden than a habit, and I started avoiding it more.
Q5: The video lectures were probably the most useful because I could pause and rewind when I needed to really understand something. As a kinesthetic learner, I'd often take notes by hand while watching, which helped me process the information better. The discussion posts were also helpful when other students explained concepts in different ways—sometimes their explanations clicked better than the instructor's.
Q6: I often skipped the supplementary readings and extra resources because I simply didn't have time. With 20 courses and limited hours, I had to prioritize what was graded. The optional practice quizzes I mostly ignored too, even though I knew they'd probably help. It wasn't that I didn't value them—I just couldn't stretch myself any thinner.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to an assignment or exam. If I could see how it would help my grade, I'd make the effort. I also valued anything that let me physically do something—work through problems, create something, practice hands-on. Pure reading or passive listening felt less worthwhile because it didn't stick as well for me.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. Required ones had deadlines and consequences, so they automatically took priority. I'd look at optional stuff and think 'that would be nice to do' but then reality would set in and I'd focus on what was mandatory. There was always this guilt about not doing more, but I had to be realistic about my limits.
Q9: Absolutely, a lot of my studying happened offline. I'd download PDFs and print them out when I could afford to, then mark them up with highlighters and write notes in the margins. I also used YouTube videos to supplement concepts I didn't understand. My actual learning was much more physical and hands-on than anything the LMS could capture.
Q10: I'd often watch a video once on the LMS, take handwritten notes, and then study from those notes for hours without logging back in. Or I'd work through practice problems on paper, talking through the steps out loud to myself. Group study sessions with classmates happened in person too—we'd quiz each other and explain concepts, but none of that showed up anywhere online.
Q11: Yes, this was pretty common for me. I'd access a lecture or reading once, download or take notes on it, and then never open it in the LMS again. My real engagement happened with the physical materials I created from that initial access. The LMS would show one view, but I might have spent five or six hours with that content offline.
Q12: Around midterms, everything got really hard. I was scared—genuinely scared—that I was going to fail despite putting in effort. The workload across all my courses peaked at the same time, and I felt paralyzed. I'd sit down to study and my mind would go blank with anxiety. The CBT session I had helped a bit, but the fear never fully went away.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from work or overwhelmed by competing deadlines, I'd just do the bare minimum on the LMS. I'd skim instead of really reading, or watch videos at 2x speed just to technically complete them. My limited resources meant I couldn't always access things when I needed to—sometimes I'd have to wait until I could get to campus for reliable internet.
Q14: There were many times I'd open materials and stare at them without really absorbing anything. My eyes would move across the screen but my brain was somewhere else, worrying about bills or other assignments. I'd technically log the time but I wasn't actually learning. Those sessions felt like going through the motions just to feel like I was doing something.
Q15: I postponed the bigger assignments constantly, even though I knew they mattered most. The fear of not doing them well enough made me avoid starting them. It's like I was scared to confirm that I couldn't handle it, so I'd do smaller tasks instead and feel productive. By the time I finally started the big ones, I didn't have enough time to do them justice.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to submit work. I'd have a burst of LMS activity right before something was due—downloading materials, watching videos I'd put off, frantically trying to complete things. It wasn't healthy, but without that pressure, I'd keep pushing things back. The deadline was the external force that overcame my avoidance.
Q17: When I got good feedback on assignments, it motivated me to engage more with similar content. But honestly, feedback often came too late to help with the next thing. And when feedback was critical, it sometimes made me more scared to try again. The CBT session helped me reframe some of that, but the fear of negative feedback still affected how quickly I'd engage with new assignments.
Q18: The course structure felt overwhelming with so much content spread across modules. I appreciated having everything organized in one place, but the sheer volume was intimidating. Weekly modules helped somewhat because they broke things into chunks, but I still struggled to keep pace. More structured checkpoints might have helped me stay on track instead of falling behind.
Q19: The lack of hands-on, interactive elements made engagement harder for me. I learn best by doing, not by watching or reading. When there were activities that let me practice and apply concepts, I engaged much more deeply. But a lot of the course was passive consumption, which didn't match my learning style. Also, the assignment instructions were sometimes unclear, which added to my anxiety.
Q20: I think those students might be like me—scared and trying to prepare but struggling to actually complete things. They might be accessing everything, trying to understand, but then getting stuck when it comes to producing work. Or maybe they're dealing with perfectionism, where they keep reviewing materials but can't bring themselves to submit something imperfect. The activity shows effort, just not output.
Q21: That actually doesn't really match my experience since I had low activity AND didn't perform well. But I can see how it happens—if someone is efficient or studies mostly offline like I do, they might not need to log in much. They could be using textbooks, study groups, or other resources. The LMS isn't the only place learning happens, especially for kinesthetic learners who need physical engagement.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, what you click on, how long you spend. But they miss the quality of that time and everything that happens offline. They can't see that I printed something and studied it for hours, or that I was staring at the screen in a panic without absorbing anything. They show behavior but not understanding or emotional state.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about and connecting with the material—not just completing tasks but genuinely trying to learn and understand. It's when I'm curious, asking questions, making connections to other things I know. For me, it's physical too—writing, practicing, doing something with my hands. It's not just about showing up; it's about being mentally and emotionally present.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how scared I was throughout this course, or how much that fear affected everything. It wouldn't show the hours I spent with printed notes, the study sessions with classmates, or the times I cried from stress. It wouldn't capture that I was working a job while taking 20 courses with limited resources. My engagement was complicated by circumstances the data just can't see.
